sections.heading: Introduction
paragraphs: The classification of attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) using eye-tracking data has traditionally relied on hand-engineered features such as saccadic latency, fixation duration, and blink rate. While these feature-based approaches have demonstrated moderate diagnostic accuracy, they are inherently limited by the researcher's prior assumptions about which gaze parameters are clinically relevant. End-to-end deep learning models offer an alternative paradigm that learns discriminative patterns directly from raw gaze coordinates without manual feature engineering.

Recent advances in deep learning for time-series classification—particularly fully convolutional networks and attention-based architectures—have achieved state-of-the-art results on multivariate physiological time-series benchmarks. Extending these methods to raw eye-tracking data is a natural next step. We propose that end-to-end deep learning models trained on raw gaze coordinates can outperform traditional feature-based classifiers for ADHD screening [hyp_001].

Furthermore, we hypothesize that a convolutional neural network operating on spatiotemporal gaze heatmaps—representing the spatial distribution of gaze over time—can achieve >85% accuracy in ADHD versus control classification without requiring manual feature engineering [hyp_002]. This approach exploits the spatial structure of visual attention patterns, which may capture subtle differences in attentional deployment that are lost when gaze data is reduced to summary statistics.

arguments.premise: Deep learning models have achieved state-of-the-art results on multivariate physiological time-series classification benchmarks.
arguments.claim: End-to-end deep learning models trained on raw eye-tracking gaze coordinates can outperform hand-engineered feature-based classifiers for ADHD screening.

arguments.premise: Gaze heatmaps capture the spatial distribution of visual attention, which may preserve subtle diagnostic patterns lost in summary statistics.
arguments.claim: A CNN operating on spatiotemporal gaze heatmaps can achieve >85% accuracy for ADHD classification.
